title: Lesson 348
transcript: Bye. One more quick video here to finish up our discussion of the authorization philosophies. As you may know, there's a certain type of question on the exam known as a drag and drop question, where you're given a list of terms and a list of definitions, and you have to line the appropriate term up with the appropriate definition. This is a really good example of a drag-and-drop question. So this is just a summary of all the different authorization philosophies that we've gone through. And I just wanted to highlight for you here kind of the key defining characteristic of each of these authorization philosophies. In case you see a question like this, it'll make it a lot easier for you to answer. So remember, the defining characteristic of discretionary access control means that the owner decides who gets access to what. And this is absolutely what we want, of course, right? Because our owners are accountable for the system, it should ultimately be the owner that's deciding who gets access to what in their system. Role-based access control means, of course, where you create roles and then you assign those roles to users and it's the role that defines what the user gets access to. rule-based access control is just a file with a set of rules in it. That file is often referred to as an ACL, an access control list, and it'll just have rules in it. You know, this person, Bob, gets access to this directory, Alice gets access to this directory, etc. Attribute or sometimes referred to as context-based access control is where you're making a much more informed decision as to who should get access to what by looking at a whole bunch of different attributes. And then of course, mandatory access control means the system decides who gets access to what. And a really important part, a really important requirement of mandatory access control is that it requires labeling. Because recall, labeling is how you mark the classification on an asset. And, of course, mandatory access control is where the system decides who gets access to what. How can the system decide? Well, it means that every single employee has to be given a security clearance, and every single asset has to be given a classification. And based on those security clearances and classifications, the system can decide who gets access to what. And that's why mandatory access control requires labeling. So there you go. Quick summary of the access authorization philosophies.
